image.caption: The visual keeps source, preview result, and inspection focus in one context.

sections.heading: Copyable example
paragraphs: Start with a minimal snippet rather than a full production file. Small input makes it easier to see whether the expression, path, config key, or SEO directive is wrong.
The snippet below can be pasted into the matching tool and then replaced with real project input.

code.caption: Get the preview working first, then expand the input.

sections.heading: Inspection checklist
paragraphs: First check whether the result is empty, then whether it matched the wrong node, field, or path. For configuration tools, pay special attention to empty values, missing variables, duplicate keys, and production defaults.
For SEO files, syntax is only the first layer. Check whether robots.txt blocks the target URL, whether sitemap XML has duplicate URLs, and whether Open Graph images are stable.

sections.heading: Publishing and SEO notes
paragraphs: Do not turn the article into a thin “this is an online tool” page. Add real snippets, mistake cases, comparisons, and screenshots so it reads like a debugging note.
Cross-link tools and articles: tool pages capture high-intent keywords, while articles capture tutorial keywords. This is healthier than generating many thin one-tool posts.

config-preview-env-yaml-toml-before-depl.title: Preview .env, YAML, TOML, and Nginx config before deployment breaks
config-preview-env-yaml-toml-before-depl.seoTitle: Preview .env, YAML, TOML, and Nginx config before deployment breaks - DiagramPreview
config-preview-env-yaml-toml-before-depl.description: A deployment-focused configuration preview guide with .env diff checks, YAML path lookups, TOML structure review, Nginx location matching, and production mistake examples.
config-preview-env-yaml-toml-before-depl.excerpt: A deployment-focused configuration preview guide with .env diff checks, YAML path lookups, TOML structure review, Nginx location matching, and production mistake examples.

sections.heading: Config bugs are cheap to preview and expensive to deploy
paragraphs: Configuration problems often look small in code review and expensive in production. One missing environment variable, one stale image tag, one TOML default, or one Nginx location rule can break a release.
A good config article should show readers how to preview those files together before deployment, not after the incident starts.

image.alt: Config preview before deployment
image.caption: A practical deployment check combines .env diff, YAML path lookup, TOML review, and Nginx route matching.

sections.heading: Demo 1: compare .env and .env.example
paragraphs: The fastest win is checking whether runtime variables match the example file. Missing keys are obvious, but empty production values and duplicate keys are just as dangerous.
This demo catches a common launch problem: the example file documents a variable that the actual environment never sets.

code.content:
DATABASE_URL=postgres://local
NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=https://example.com
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=
---
DATABASE_URL=
NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=
WEBHOOK_SECRET=
code.caption: The .env diff should flag WEBHOOK_SECRET as missing and STRIPE_SECRET_KEY as empty.

sections.heading: Demo 2: inspect the exact YAML value you deploy
paragraphs: Kubernetes, Helm, and GitHub Actions files are usually too large to inspect line by line. A YAML path preview lets you ask one focused question: which image, branch, CPU limit, or secret name will this deploy use?
This is more useful than a generic YAML tree when the review is about one production value.

code.content:
spec.template.spec.containers[0].image
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: example/api:latest
code.caption: If production still points at latest, call that out before the release.

sections.heading: Demo 3: review TOML sections and defaults
paragraphs: TOML often hides in pyproject, Cargo, config files, and internal tools. It is readable, but defaults can still be wrong for production.
A structure preview helps reviewers scan sections, keys, and values without learning the whole application.

code.content:
[server]
port = 3000
workers = 1

[features]
search = true
new_checkout = false
code.caption: The article should explain which TOML values are safe defaults and which need environment-specific review.

sections.heading: Demo 4: test Nginx location matching
paragraphs: Nginx routing bugs are easy to miss because exact, prefix, ^~, and regex locations have different precedence. A route can look correct in the file and still match a different block.
Preview the target URI and the relevant location blocks before deploying a rewrite or proxy rule.

code.caption: The expected result is ^~ /api/, not the fallback location.

sections.heading: Mistakes this checklist catches
paragraphs: A secret is documented but not present. A deployment uses latest instead of an immutable image tag. A TOML worker count is safe locally but too low in production. A regex route steals traffic from a prefix route. Cache headers make clients keep a stale bundle.
Those concrete failures make the article useful for readers who are preparing a release.

sections.heading: When not to rely on the preview alone
paragraphs: Do not use a preview as a replacement for staging deployment, secret scanning, or runtime health checks. The preview is for catching obvious mismatches early.
The final release check should still include real environment variables, deployed manifests, Nginx reload validation, and application logs.

technical-seo-preview-robots-sitemap-ope.title: Technical SEO preview workflow: robots.txt, sitemap.xml, Open Graph, and SVG covers
technical-seo-preview-robots-sitemap-ope.seoTitle: Technical SEO preview workflow: robots.txt, sitemap.xml, Open Graph, and SVG covers - DiagramPreview
technical-seo-preview-robots-sitemap-ope.description: A technical SEO preview guide for checking robots.txt rules, sitemap XML quality, hreflang alternates, Open Graph cards, SVG covers, and publish-ready screenshots.
technical-seo-preview-robots-sitemap-ope.excerpt: A technical SEO preview guide for checking robots.txt rules, sitemap XML quality, hreflang alternates, Open Graph cards, SVG covers, and publish-ready screenshots.

sections.heading: Technical SEO files need previews too
paragraphs: Small technical SEO files can create large indexing problems. A robots.txt rule can block a whole section, a sitemap can duplicate thousands of URLs, and an Open Graph image can fail silently until the page is already shared.
A strong article should show the publish workflow: robots rule check, sitemap inspection, Open Graph preview, SVG cover review, then final launch checks.

image.alt: Technical SEO preview workflow
image.caption: The technical SEO preview workflow connects crawl rules, sitemap quality, social cards, and generated cover images.

sections.heading: Demo 1: test one URL against robots.txt
paragraphs: Do not review robots.txt only as text. Pick the exact URL you want indexed and test whether the longest matching Allow or Disallow rule permits it.
This is especially important after adding private dashboards, API routes, staging paths, or generated blog directories.

code.content:
URL: https://example.com/blog/jq-vs-jsonpath-api-debugging-workflow
---
User-agent: *
Disallow: /admin/
Disallow: /api/
Allow: /blog/
Sitemap: https://example.com/sitemap.xml
code.caption: The expected result is allowed for /blog while /admin and /api stay blocked.

sections.heading: Demo 2: inspect sitemap URLs and alternates
paragraphs: A sitemap preview should show counts, duplicates, lastmod values, sitemap indexes, and hreflang alternates. The goal is not just validating XML. The goal is seeing whether the sitemap matches the site architecture.
For a multilingual tool site, hreflang alternates are as important as URL count. Broken alternates can send search engines mixed signals.

code.content:
<urlset xmlns="http://www.sitemaps.org/schemas/sitemap/0.9" xmlns:xhtml="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
  <url>
    <loc>https://example.com/en/jq-filter-tester</loc>
    <xhtml:link rel="alternate" hreflang="zh-CN" href="https://example.com/zh-CN/jq-filter-tester" />
    <xhtml:link rel="alternate" hreflang="ja" href="https://example.com/ja/jq-filter-tester" />
  </url>
</urlset>
code.caption: The sitemap article should explain URL count, alternates, duplicate URLs, and lastmod quality.

sections.heading: Demo 3: preview Open Graph before sharing
paragraphs: Open Graph issues are easy to miss because the HTML can look correct while the image URL is too large, blocked, redirected, or visually unclear.
Preview title, description, image, URL, and site name before posting a launch note. This is more useful than checking meta tags manually.

code.content:
<meta property="og:title" content="jq vs JSONPath: API response debugging workflow" />
<meta property="og:description" content="A practical guide with jq filters, JSONPath selectors, and copyable API payload demos." />
<meta property="og:image" content="https://diagrampreview.com/blog/jq-vs-jsonpath-api-debugging-workflow.png" />
<meta property="og:url" content="https://diagrampreview.com/en/blog/jq-vs-jsonpath-api-debugging-workflow" />
code.caption: The OG card should describe the real tutorial, not just the product name.

sections.heading: Demo 4: inspect SVG cover images
paragraphs: Generated cover images and SVG snippets should be previewed before publishing. Broken text, invisible contrast, scripts, or huge dimensions can make a good article look untrustworthy.
Use SVG preview for code-generated covers and keep PNG exports for platforms that do not handle SVG well.

sections.heading: Common SEO preview mistakes
paragraphs: Submitting a sitemap before checking duplicate URLs. Blocking /api/ and accidentally blocking /api-docs/. Publishing social cards with a local image URL. Forgetting hreflang alternates for new localized tool pages. Adding many blog pages but not linking them from relevant tools.
These are practical mistakes that a reader can immediately compare with their own site.

sections.heading: Launch checklist
paragraphs: Check robots.txt for the exact URL, inspect sitemap count and alternates, preview Open Graph, verify image URLs, and confirm the article links to the relevant tool pages.
After publishing, submit the sitemap or IndexNow only for pages that are useful enough to stand on their own.

script-preview-debugging-html-css-json-b.title: Проверяйте HTML, CSS, JSON и Base64 от ИИ перед публикацией
script-preview-debugging-html-css-json-b.seoTitle: Preview AI-generated HTML, CSS, JSON Diff, and Base64 images before publishing
script-preview-debugging-html-css-json-b.description: A practical developer workflow for previewing AI-generated HTML, CSS, JSON, Base64, cURL, URL query, shadow, and color snippets before they reach docs, issues, blogs, or production examples.
script-preview-debugging-html-css-json-b.excerpt: AI can generate snippets, but it does not open the browser, compare payloads, inspect requests, gradients, shadows, palettes, or embedded image size for you.

sections.heading: Why these tools belong together
paragraphs: Modern documentation and frontend snippets often come from AI output, browser consoles, API responses, copied config, or generated assets. Text can look correct while the rendered layout, JSON change, or embedded image is still wrong.
This P1 batch focuses on the step after generation and before publishing: turn scripts, snippets, and visual source into something you can inspect.

image.alt: HTML CSS JSON Base64 preview workflow
image.caption: P1 expands DiagramPreview from diagrams into script and visual-source preview workflows.

sections.heading: Demo 1: Put HTML snippets in a sandbox first
paragraphs: AI-generated landing sections, email templates, and component snippets often fail through overflow, broken mobile layout, invalid tags, or unsafe scripts. HTML Preview Sandbox renders the snippet in a script-blocked iframe.
It is not a full replacement for browser testing, but it is a very fast review surface before publishing README examples, support docs, or marketing posts.

code.content:
Preview before publish

Debug generated HTML without leaving the browser

Paste a component, email block, or landing section.
code.caption: Start with a small snippet, then expand to the full source.

sections.heading: Demo 2: CSS gradients need visual inspection
paragraphs: Gradient strings are hard to review as text. Direction, contrast, transparency, and color stops only become obvious after rendering. CSS Gradient Preview extracts gradients and shows swatches for hero sections, buttons, social cards, and screenshot backgrounds.
When AI generates visual CSS, preview the background instead of trusting the string.

code.content:
background:
  radial-gradient(circle at 20% 20%, rgba(34,197,94,.55), transparent 30%),
  radial-gradient(circle at 80% 10%, rgba(37,99,235,.55), transparent 32%),
  linear-gradient(135deg, #f8fafc, #e0f2fe);

sections.heading: Demo 3: JSON Diff beats manual comparison
paragraphs: API responses, config files, and package dependency changes should be diffed before they are documented. After an AI edit, added fields, removed fields, and array changes are easy to miss.
JSON Diff Viewer separates the before and after payloads with --- and reports added, removed, and changed paths.

sections.heading: Demo 4: Base64 images are convenient, not always healthy
paragraphs: Base64 images often appear inside SVG, email templates, JSON config, and Open Graph drafts. They are easy to copy, but they make files larger, caching weaker, and code review harder.
Base64 Image Preview confirms whether the image renders, whether the MIME type looks right, and whether the encoded size is too large.

code.content: data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0i...

sections.heading: Demo 5: cURL and URL parameters deserve a preview too
paragraphs: Many API issues are not backend bugs. They come from request commands, headers, query parameters, or encoding. cURL Command Parser breaks a command into method, URL, headers, and body; URL Query Parser is useful for UTM fields, search parameters, and encoded redirects.
This is practical for tickets and support replies: paste the original command and the parsed screenshot so teammates do not have to guess what the request looked like.

code.content:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/orders?debug=true \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer demo-token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data-raw '{"sku":"A1","qty":2}'

sections.heading: Demo 6: Visual CSS needs review screenshots
paragraphs: Shadows and colors are often treated as small finishing touches, but they directly affect perceived quality, readability, and social screenshots. CSS Box Shadow Preview renders layered shadows on cards, while Color Palette Preview checks color tokens and adjacent contrast.
If AI generates a theme or card style, do not copy the CSS blindly. Render the visual output first, then decide whether it belongs in the site.

sections.heading: A practical publishing checklist
paragraphs: Preview HTML structure first, inspect CSS gradient output, diff JSON payload changes, then check embedded Base64 images.
If the snippet came from AI, put both the source and the preview screenshot in the PR or documentation draft so teammates can review and reproduce the result.

about.title: About
about.description: DiagramPreview is an online diagram preview and conversion workspace for developers, product teams, architects, and technical writers.

sections.heading: What DiagramPreview is
paragraphs: DiagramPreview helps people turn text, structured data, and diagram files into visual previews that are easier to inspect, share, and export.
The site is built for Mermaid, PlantUML, Markdown, Graphviz, D2, draw.io, mind maps, JSON, YAML, and future developer diagram formats.

sections.heading: How the product is designed
paragraphs: The first screen focuses on tools, search, and categories instead of a marketing-only landing page. Each tool has its own URL, metadata, related links, FAQ, and export workflow.

help-center.title: Help Center
help-center.description: Find help for using DiagramPreview tools, previews, exports, and common diagram workflows.

sections.heading: Using tools
paragraphs: Open a tool, paste source content, choose an example when useful, and review the preview pane for output or syntax errors.
Supported tools expose copy, SVG, PNG, and print-style PDF actions when the renderer can provide them.

sections.heading: Troubleshooting
paragraphs: If a diagram does not render, reduce the source to a smaller example, check the syntax for the format, and then add sections back gradually.
For PlantUML, the first version uses a PlantUML server URL, so network access to that endpoint may affect previews.

contact.title: Contact
contact.description: Contact DiagramPreview for feedback, support, partnerships, and product questions.

sections.heading: Email
paragraphs: For feedback, write to zplviewercontact@gmail.com. For general contact, write to zplviewercontact@gmail.com.

sections.heading: What to include
paragraphs: Please include the tool URL, the diagram format, a short description of the issue, and whether the problem affects preview, export, localization, or SEO content.

sections.heading: Data handling
paragraphs: DiagramPreview is designed as a browser-first tool site. Where possible, rendering happens in the browser rather than requiring diagram content to be sent to a backend.
Some third-party rendering approaches, such as the first PlantUML server URL implementation, may request a remote preview image.

sections.heading: Contact
paragraphs: For privacy questions, contact zplviewercontact@gmail.com. This page is a practical product policy page and should be reviewed by legal counsel before commercial launch.

terms-of-use.title: Terms of Use
terms-of-use.description: Terms for using DiagramPreview tools and content.

sections.heading: Use of the service
paragraphs: DiagramPreview provides online tools for previewing, converting, and exporting diagrams. You are responsible for the content you paste, upload, preview, or export.
Do not use the service to process content you are not allowed to use or share.

sections.heading: No warranty
paragraphs: The tools are provided as-is. Renderers, exports, examples, and documentation may change as the product evolves.
This page is a practical starting point and should be reviewed by legal counsel before commercial launch.
